EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough and associated cold front move through the region and brought a round of severe weather, mainly to the northern portion of the forecast area. Hail and some wind damage occurred along and north of the Highway 82 corridor during the evening of the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds downed one tree which fell on a power line which then caused a house fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough and associated cold front move through the region and brought a round of severe weather, mainly to the northern portion of the forecast area. Hail and some wind damage occurred along and north of the Highway 82 corridor during the evening of the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swath of quarter to golf ball sized hail fell across a portion of Central Bolivar county. Quarter sized hail moved into the Beulah Community from across the river and tracked eastward into Cleveland. The hail grew up to golf ball size around and just west of Cleveland. The hail dissipated just east of Cleveland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across the region and evolved into a line of storms as it moved across the area. This system did produce a little severe weather, but was mainly a rain producer and help relieve some of the drought across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado touched down just southwest of Moorehead, in a farm field, and leveled crops before reaching Moorehead. As the tornado moved into the southwest portion of town, a number of trees were snapped, a barn had a portion of the roof blown off and a a few outbuildings were damaged. Additionally, a metal gazebo was thrown onto a house and a metal walkway was destroyed at a school.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon and early evening of November 21st, a storm system and cold front moved across the region. Instability was limited and only isolated severe storms occurred.
A house was struck by lightning near Wiggins.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A home was struck by lightning in Wiggins.  The strike caused a fire in the hot water heater.  The fire was contained to a small area.  The home still suffered considerable smoke and water damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through North Mississippi during the afternoon hours of November 14, 2007. Showers and thunderstorms accompanied the front through the area. Some of the storms produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding on County Road 614, outside of Corinth. Two vehicles were underwater as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through North Mississippi during the afternoon hours of November 14, 2007. Showers and thunderstorms accompanied the front through the area. Some of the storms produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong gradient winds out ahead of a cold front knocked down a few power poles and power lines near Independence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong to severe thunderstorms developed in an unstable atmosphere.

By the last week of April, dry conditions persisted and the area categorized by severe drought was expanded. This new area moved further south and reached down to Highway 84. It was also expanded westward, generally to the Mississippi River, but excluded the Mississippi Delta land. During these last few days of April, rainfall deficits were now running between 10 and 14 inches below normal. Again, areas across Eastern Mississippi remained the driest.|Even as the drought conditions were classified as severe, area farmers were not suffering and were only experiencing small impacts of the dry weather. This is mainly due to the fact that the soil still had a little moisture left from the winter rains and during the second half of April, a few days did receive some small amounts of rain, which at least introduced some moisture to the very top of the soil.

A strong storm system moved across the Plains and Mid-West late on April 3rd and during the morning hours of April 4th. In advance of this system, scattered severe thunderstorms developed over Mississippi during the afternoon hours of April 3rd. This activity only lasted for a few hours during the late afternoon. However, across the Mid-South region, numerous severe thunderstorms developed and pushed south through the evening. After midnight, these storms began to weaken, but several remained severe as the complex pushed south across Mississippi. Scattered severe storms continued until around 4 am before the complex dissipated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to golf ball sized hail fell across the western portion of Marion County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level disturbance lifted northeast across the Southern Plains and into the Ohio Valley. As this system evolved and tracked northeast, severe weather broke out across a large portion of the region between April 13th and 14th. Many reports of large hail, damaging winds and a few isolated tornadoes were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down, some in the city and others across the county.

The spring of 2007 started a bit early with the region seeing its first severe weather outbreak on February 24th. A large and powerful storm system took shape as a deep surface low tracked across Kansas and into portions of Iowa. This strong area of low pressure was driven by a powerful upper level trough which supported very strong winds through the entire atmosphere. These winds were felt at the surface to some degree as a large area across the Lower Mississippi River Valley saw sustained winds between 25-35 mph with gusts between 40-50 mph. Those gradient winds, in advance of the severe weather, were strong enough to down some trees and power lines across the region. Those damage reports were more scattered in nature.|Those strong winds from the deepening surface low helped to draw northward moisture and instability. The strong winds allowed for the environment to become highly sheared. Basically, there were increasing winds with height and a change in the wind direction as well. This particular combination of instability and high shear was quite rare. However, this set the stage for a severe weather outbreak which included numerous reports of damaging winds, large hail and a few tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees  and a few power lines were blown down across the county. One of the downed power lines started a fire in Preston. This damage was caused during the strong gradient wind gusts in advance of the line of storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms ahead of strong cold front moved across southeast Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds from a thunderstorm blew down several trees and caused roof damage to a couple of homes.

During these last few days of April, rainfall deficits were now running between 10 and 14 inches below normal. Again, areas across Eastern Mississippi remained the driest.|Even as the drought conditions were classified as severe, area farmers were not suffering and were only experiencing small impacts of the dry weather. This is mainly due to the fact that the soil still had a little moisture left from the winter rains and during the second half of April, a few days did receive some small amounts of rain, which at least introduced some moisture to the very top of the soil.
A thunderstorm squall line moved across southeast Mississippi damaging trees and power lines.

A strong storm system moved across the Plains and Mid-West late on April 3rd and during the morning hours of April 4th. In advance of this system, scattered severe thunderstorms developed over Mississippi during the afternoon hours of April 3rd. This activity only lasted for a few hours during the late afternoon. However, across the Mid-South region, numerous severe thunderstorms developed and pushed south through the evening. After midnight, these storms began to weaken, but several remained severe as the complex pushed south across Mississippi. Scattered severe storms continued until around 4 am before the complex dissipated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down.

A potent upper level disturbance lifted northeast across the Southern Plains and into the Ohio Valley. As this system evolved and tracked northeast, severe weather broke out across a large portion of the region between April 13th and 14th. Many reports of large hail, damaging winds and a few isolated tornadoes were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down, some across roads in the Learned Community.

The spring of 2007 started a bit early with the region seeing its first severe weather outbreak on February 24th. A large and powerful storm system took shape as a deep surface low tracked across Kansas and into portions of Iowa. This strong area of low pressure was driven by a powerful upper level trough which supported very strong winds through the entire atmosphere. These winds were felt at the surface to some degree as a large area across the Lower Mississippi River Valley saw sustained winds between 25-35 mph with gusts between 40-50 mph. Those gradient winds, in advance of the severe weather, were strong enough to down some trees and power lines across the region. Those damage reports were more scattered in nature.|Those strong winds from the deepening surface low helped to draw northward moisture and instability. The strong winds allowed for the environment to become highly sheared. Basically, there were increasing winds with height and a change in the wind direction as well. This particular combination of instability and high shear was quite rare. However, this set the stage for a severe weather outbreak which included numerous reports of damaging winds, large hail and a few tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another powerful spring storm system developed over the plains and spread eastward causing severe weather from the Mid West to the south-central Gulf Coast states to the Southeast United States. Overall, the Lower Mississippi Valley was spared the brunt of the worst weather with just scattered reports of severe weather. However, the  Southeast United States was hit quite hard as significant severe storms occurred. This region had many reports of large hail and damaging winds with several strong tornadoes (EF2 to EF3) and a few violent tornadoes (EF4).

A large and powerful storm system took shape as a deep surface low tracked across Kansas and into portions of Iowa. This strong area of low pressure was driven by a powerful upper level trough which supported very strong winds through the entire atmosphere. These winds were felt at the surface to some degree as a large area across the Lower Mississippi River Valley saw sustained winds between 25-35 mph with gusts between 40-50 mph. Those gradient winds, in advance of the severe weather, were strong enough to down some trees and power lines across the region. Those damage reports were more scattered in nature.|Those strong winds from the deepening surface low helped to draw northward moisture and instability. The strong winds allowed for the environment to become highly sheared. Basically, there were increasing winds with height and a change in the wind direction as well. This particular combination of instability and high shear was quite rare. However, this set the stage for a severe weather outbreak which included numerous reports of damaging winds, large hail and a few tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered trees and power lines blown down across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong to isolated severe thunderstorms moved from southeast Louisiana during the late evening into coastal Mississippi during the early morning house.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriffs Office reported that a trailer was overturned by thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another powerful spring storm system developed over the plains and spread eastward causing severe weather from the Mid West to the south-central Gulf Coast states to the Southeast United States. Overall, the Lower Mississippi Valley was spared the brunt of the worst weather with just scattered reports of severe weather. However, the  Southeast United States was hit quite hard as significant severe storms occurred. This region had many reports of large hail and damaging winds with several strong tornadoes (EF2 to EF3) and a few violent tornadoes (EF4).

These winds were felt at the surface to some degree as a large area across the Lower Mississippi River Valley saw sustained winds between 25-35 mph with gusts between 40-50 mph. Those gradient winds, in advance of the severe weather, were strong enough to down some trees and power lines across the region. Those damage reports were more scattered in nature.|Those strong winds from the deepening surface low helped to draw northward moisture and instability. The strong winds allowed for the environment to become highly sheared. Basically, there were increasing winds with height and a change in the wind direction as well. This particular combination of instability and high shear was quite rare. However, this set the stage for a severe weather outbreak which included numerous reports of damaging winds, large hail and a few tornadoes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado developed during the early morning hours over Stone county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado first touched down near the fire station in Wiggins.  The tornado did some minor roof damage to the station.  The tornado then moved across the street and blew the roof off of the old train depot.  The tornado then moved across a business where several metal buildings were damaged.  The tornado then went back into the clouds just northeast of the business.  Some debris from the train depot was found about a quarter of a mile away near Flint Creek Water Park.  No one was injured.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado touched down 6 miles southwest of Indianola and raced northeast toward and across the southern part of town. Numerous trees were snapped along with a few power poles and metal signs blown down. Several sheds were also destroyed along the path. The Kingdom Hall Church, along Highway 49, had half of its roof blown off along with the entire car port destroyed. An area fence was severe damaged as debris was blown along the path. An irrigation pivot system was flipped over and severely damaged just east of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours of February 13th, a line of strong and severe storms moved across southern and central Mississippi. This line produce hail, wind damage a two weak tornadoes as it moved across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This weak tornado touched down near the intersection of Highway 35 and County Road 504. One home had some roofing blown off, power poles were blown down and a few trees were snapped. The tornado tracked along the north side of Highway 504 downing many trees. When it crossed Highway 504 it uprooted and snapped a number of large pine trees, snapped two power poles and damaged a porch. The tornado then traced through forested land downing dozens of trees. It dissipated just before crossing Dixon Road where it caused minor roof damage to a chicken house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another powerful spring storm system developed over the plains and spread eastward causing severe weather from the Mid West to the south-central Gulf Coast states to the Southeast United States. Overall, the Lower Mississippi Valley was spared the brunt of the worst weather with just scattered reports of severe weather. However, the  Southeast United States was hit quite hard as significant severe storms occurred. This region had many reports of large hail and damaging winds with several strong tornadoes (EF2 to EF3) and a few violent tornadoes (EF4).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado touched down near Dale Road and Wildwood Road and ended near Highway 432 and Walker Road. Numerous trees were down along with roof damage to a barn near Luce Road. Numerous trees and power lines were blown down across Noblin Road and again near Highway 432 and Walker Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass moved over much of North Mississippi during the early morning hours of April 8, 2007. Record-breaking low temperatures occurred over the area as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Record-breaking low temperatures occurred the morning of April 8, 2007. Lows dropped into the mid to upper 20s which caused considerable crop and vegetation damage. Leaves were literally frozen off of trees. Corn that had six to seven leaves or beyond was killed. Winter wheat suffered the most harm. 75 to 80 percent of the winter wheat crop was destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below normal rainfall during the month of April brought severe drought conditions to Northeast Mississippi which expanded to North Central Mississippi by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe drought(D2), which began in early April across portions of Northeast Mississippi expanded to North Central Mississippi by the end of April. Rainfall was about 3 inches below normal for the month bringing the yearly deficit to around 9 inches below normal. The drought impacted agricultural and hydrological interests of the area.

Rainfall was about 3 inches below normal for the month bringing the yearly deficit to around 9 inches below normal. The drought impacted agricultural and hydrological interests of the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved into North Mississippi during the late afternoon and evening hours of April 3, 2007. The front produced numerous showers and thunderstorms many of which became severe. Damaging winds and large hail occurred as a result.

A strong cold front moved into North Mississippi during the late afternoon and evening hours of April 3, 2007. The front produced numerous showers and thunderstorms many of which became severe. Damaging winds and large hail occurred as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong gradient winds knocked down a few trees in Burton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The spring of 2007 started a bit early with the region seeing its first severe weather outbreak on February 24th. A large and powerful storm system took shape as a deep surface low tracked across Kansas and into portions of Iowa. This strong area of low pressure was driven by a powerful upper level trough which supported very strong winds through the entire atmosphere. These winds were felt at the surface to some degree as a large area across the Lower Mississippi River Valley saw sustained winds between 25-35 mph with gusts between 40-50 mph. Those gradient winds, in advance of the severe weather, were strong enough to down some trees and power lines across the region. Those damage reports were more scattered in nature.|Those strong winds from the deepening surface low helped to draw northward moisture and instability. The strong winds allowed for the environment to become highly sheared. Basically, there were increasing winds with height and a change in the wind direction as well. This particular combination of instability and high shear was quite rare. However, this set the stage for a severe weather outbreak which included numerous reports of damaging winds, large hail and a few tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were blown down off Highway 98.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong thunderstorms developed across coastal Mississippi as a weakening cold front moved into the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed across south Mississippi in advance of a strong cold front that moved across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down around the communities of Wade and Hurley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed across south Mississippi in advance of a strong cold front that moved across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex storm system took shape at the end of December and brought a round of elevated hail storms to the region. The hail storms occurred as a warm front and deeper moisture surged northward during the early morning hours, generally between 1 and 6 am and were scattered about the forecast area. The hail stones ranged between penny and quarter size and caused little to no damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swath of penny to quarter size hail fell across a portion of Marion County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small, but potent, storm system evolved and moved east across the region during the first half of the day. A large MCV (meso-scale convective vortex) developed across Central Louisiana during the morning hours and moved east adding an extra kick to the atmosphere and aided in generating a line of severe thunderstorms which produced five tornadoes and wind damage. All the severe weather was confined to the southern areas, generally along Highway 84, and extended from Natchez to Brookhaven to Laurel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado began as a small, narrow path of minor damage, including a porch being blown off a house. It reached its maximum intensity as it crossed highway 29. Here, a brick home had all of its roof structure blown off along with a few walls blown down. Three mobile homes were rolled or tossed and destroyed, with debris strewn downstream along the path. Numerous trees were snapped off or uprooted. The last damage occurred along Leroy Hill Road. A home suffered significant roof damage, an outbuilding was destroyed, a car suffered major damage, and numerous large trees were snapped or uprooted. The tornado dissipated shortly after crossing Leroy Hill Road with only a few trees snapped at the end of the path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small, but potent, storm system evolved and moved east across the region during the first half of the day. A large MCV (meso-scale convective vortex) developed across Central Louisiana during the morning hours and moved east adding an extra kick to the atmosphere and aided in generating a line of severe thunderstorms which produced five tornadoes and wind damage. All the severe weather was confined to the southern areas, generally along Highway 84, and extended from Natchez to Brookhaven to Laurel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This strong tornado touched down just east of the Brookhaven-Lincoln County Airport and tracked east-northeast for five miles to near the Woolworth Community. The most significant damage occurred just south of Heucks Crossing along Beeson Road where two mobile homes were obliterated. The debris was swept from foundations with large debris, including furniture, and appliances thrown more than 100 yards. Additionally, numerous trees, both pines and hardwoods, were snapped and uprooted along with several power lines down along the path. A shop was also destroyed, camper trailer heavily damaged and two other homes suffered significant roof damage.

Severe weather developed over the region during the day on May 3rd, peaked during the late afternoon hours and ended by early evening. The majority of the severe weather, as well as the hardest hit areas, occurred across Southeast Arkansas, Northeast Louisiana and West-Central Mississippi. A mix of hail, damaging winds and a few tornadoes occurred during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were blown down on the eastern side of the county near the Lawrence county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an active severe weather day on June 18th, the atmosphere quickly recovered and was able to support additional severe weather which began in the late evening and persisted through the overnight hours. Severe weather during the overnight hours in June is quite rare but does occur on occasion. One of the overnight storms actually produced a rare June tornado! This overnight severe weather was likely a sign and just a taste of what was to come during the day on June 19th. As the overnight/early morning complex of storms shifted east, conditions were set just right for additional storms to redevelop to the west by late morning. The atmosphere recovered once more and became quite unstable in the wake of the morning complex. A very potent disturbance dropped south out of Arkansas and developed a large bow echo complex which plowed south across the forecast area. This bow echo was very efficient at producing severe wind gusts and a large swath of wind damage occurred as the system moved southward across Mississippi into Southeast Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were blown down along with numerous large limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed across the southern portion of the region with a few becoming strong and one reaching severe limits.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active sea breeze front contained a northward moving line of thunderstorms which produced scattered severe storms across the region. This sea breeze front was much stronger than typical as unusually strong winds were present in the mid levels of the atmosphere. These increased winds allowed the afternoon storms to pack more of a punch as wind damage occurred as they moved north across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down on power lines. Damage was confined to the south and southwest portion of Clay County.

Severe weather during the overnight hours in June is quite rare but does occur on occasion. One of the overnight storms actually produced a rare June tornado! This overnight severe weather was likely a sign and just a taste of what was to come during the day on June 19th. As the overnight/early morning complex of storms shifted east, conditions were set just right for additional storms to redevelop to the west by late morning. The atmosphere recovered once more and became quite unstable in the wake of the morning complex. A very potent disturbance dropped south out of Arkansas and developed a large bow echo complex which plowed south across the forecast area. This bow echo was very efficient at producing severe wind gusts and a large swath of wind damage occurred as the system moved southward across Mississippi into Southeast Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down around town.

These increased winds allowed the afternoon storms to pack more of a punch as wind damage occurred as they moved north across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were blown down.

A very potent disturbance dropped south out of Arkansas and developed a large bow echo complex which plowed south across the forecast area. This bow echo was very efficient at producing severe wind gusts and a large swath of wind damage occurred as the system moved southward across Mississippi into Southeast Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sixty three MPH wind gust was measured at the Ross Barnett Reservoir tower.

As the overnight/early morning complex of storms shifted east, conditions were set just right for additional storms to redevelop to the west by late morning. The atmosphere recovered once more and became quite unstable in the wake of the morning complex. A very potent disturbance dropped south out of Arkansas and developed a large bow echo complex which plowed south across the forecast area. This bow echo was very efficient at producing severe wind gusts and a large swath of wind damage occurred as the system moved southward across Mississippi into Southeast Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down from Hattiesburg to Petal. Several power lines were blown down as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous slow moving thunderstorms developed across the area during the afternoon and evening of July 20th. A few of these became severe and produced damaging wind gusts. The slow nature of these storms also allowed them to produce locally heavy rainfall with a number of areas seeing 1 to 2 inches in a short amount of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As two large outflow boundaries collided across Central Hinds county, a large band of very slow moving thunderstorms developed. These storms produced 4 to 5 inches of rain with most of that, 2 to 3 inches, falling in an hour. This intense rainfall caused flash flooding across some of the area. Several roads were under water with several vehicles flooded up to the windows. Two people had to be rescued from their vehicle in Byram. Also in Byram, 5 homes were flooded by several feet of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month of July offered some relief from the exceptional drought which occurred during the first part of summer. However, hot and dry conditions returned in August and caused a small section of Eastern Mississippi to reach severe drought conditions once again. Luckily, ground moisture from the rains in July kept the drought from expanding or causing much in the way of agricultural losses.

The remnants, of what was briefly hurricane Humberto, moved slowly east northeast across portions of Central and Northeast Mississippi. Across Southwest Mississippi, a couple of storms became severe and produced a little wind damage. The main result from the remnants of Humberto was the locally heavy rainfall that occurred. The heaviest rains fell during the early morning hours of September 14th across Carroll, Montgomery and Webster counties. These three counties received 4 to 6 inches of rain and some flash flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees and power lines were blown down in and around Natchez.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system developed across the central sections of the country and brought two rounds of severe weather to the region. This mid October severe weather event was one of the top five largest across the United States.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants of Hurricane Humberto moved over portions of Northeast Mississippi and produced very heavy rain. Flash flooding was reported as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Railroad engineer reported many small streams were flooded along railroad line in Monroe County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong to severe thunderstorms developed in an unstable airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants, of what was briefly hurricane Humberto, moved slowly east northeast across portions of Central and Northeast Mississippi. Across Southwest Mississippi, a couple of storms became severe and produced a little wind damage. The main result from the remnants of Humberto was the locally heavy rainfall that occurred. The heaviest rains fell during the early morning hours of September 14th across Carroll, Montgomery and Webster counties. These three counties received 4 to 6 inches of rain and some flash flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding from heavy rain washed a small railroad bridge out over Muckle Creek on the north side of Jackson. Amtrak train number 29 was stopped and passengers were placed on buses to continue their trip.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rain, 4 to 6 inches, caused a portion of Myers Road to wash out. One person was injured as they drove a vehicle into the washed out road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved into North Mississippi during the afternoon and evening hours of August 30th, 2007. The front produced scattered showers and thunderstorms over the area. Some of the thunderstorms produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Canopy over a gas station in Como shook vigorously from estimated thunderstorm winds of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of Jan 4th and early morning hours of Jan 5th, a potent storm system moved across the Lower Mississippi Valley. This system was dynamic enough to generate a line of thunderstorms across Central Mississippi. This line developed within a strongly sheared environment and evolved into a squall line with bowing segments. These bowing segments proved very efficient with respect to producing damaging wind gusts and six tornadoes. The squall line matured just east of Interstate 55 around 11 pm and raced east before exiting into Alabama around 3 am.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado touched down in northwest Newton County, near the Prospect Community, and tracked north northeast into southwest Neshoba County. Four chicken houses were destroyed along with one home sustaining minor damage. This tornado moved into Neshoba County and was on the ground for another 2 miles. The total path length was 5.3 miles and was rated F1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small, but potent, storm system evolved and moved east across the region during the first half of the day. A large MCV (meso-scale convective vortex) developed across Central Louisiana during the morning hours and moved east adding an extra kick to the atmosphere and aided in generating a line of severe thunderstorms which produced five tornadoes and wind damage. All the severe weather was confined to the southern areas, generally along Highway 84, and extended from Natchez to Brookhaven to Laurel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down on power lines which knocked out power near Roxie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex storm system took shape at the end of December and brought a round of elevated hail storms to the region. The hail storms occurred as a warm front and deeper moisture surged northward during the early morning hours, generally between 1 and 6 am and were scattered about the forecast area. The hail stones ranged between penny and quarter size and caused little to no damage.

A low pressure system tracked northeast from Texas into Southern Mississippi during the afternoon and evening hours of 1/31/07. Cold air was already in place over the North Mississippi thus precipitation associated with the system fell as sleet and freezing rain. Light accumulations occurred as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light sleet fell over Tallahatchie County and produced minor accumulations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system with a trailing cold front pushed into portions of North Mississippi during the late afternoon hours of 2/24/07. Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the system and produced large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down a tree onto a house on Burkett Street in Calhoun City. In addition, other trees and powerlines were blown down in the Calhoun City area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The spring of 2007 started a bit early with the region seeing its first severe weather outbreak on February 24th. A large and powerful storm system took shape as a deep surface low tracked across Kansas and into portions of Iowa. This strong area of low pressure was driven by a powerful upper level trough which supported very strong winds through the entire atmosphere. These winds were felt at the surface to some degree as a large area across the Lower Mississippi River Valley saw sustained winds between 25-35 mph with gusts between 40-50 mph. Those gradient winds, in advance of the severe weather, were strong enough to down some trees and power lines across the region. Those damage reports were more scattered in nature.|Those strong winds from the deepening surface low helped to draw northward moisture and instability. The strong winds allowed for the environment to become highly sheared. Basically, there were increasing winds with height and a change in the wind direction as well. This particular combination of instability and high shear was quite rare. However, this set the stage for a severe weather outbreak which included numerous reports of damaging winds, large hail and a few tornadoes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another powerful spring storm system developed over the plains and spread eastward causing severe weather from the Mid West to the south-central Gulf Coast states to the Southeast United States. Overall, the Lower Mississippi Valley was spared the brunt of the worst weather with just scattered reports of severe weather. However, the  Southeast United States was hit quite hard as significant severe storms occurred. This region had many reports of large hail and damaging winds with several strong tornadoes (EF2 to EF3) and a few violent tornadoes (EF4).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This system generated early morning thunderstorms across Northern Louisiana, some of these were severe, which then spread a decaying outflow boundary north-eastward across the Mississippi Delta and into North-Central Mississippi before dissipating. This complex of storms and then outflow boundary caused wind damage across portions of far Southeast Arkansas, Northeast Louisiana and portions of Mississippi. Additional isolated severe thunderstorms occurred later in the afternoon before the entire system exited to the northeast.|As the outflow boundary moved across Grenada County, wind were strong enough to blow over a mobile home being towed along Interstate 55. This gustnado downed a few trees while briefly on the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few power lines were blown down at Wilcox Road and Highway 1, just south of Greenville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Across a large portion of Mississippi, drier than normal conditions began taking shape by late winter which persisted into early spring. By the middle of April, a large portion of Eastern and some of Central Mississippi was classified as having severe drought conditions. This area generally extended from the Jackson Metro area and points to the northeast. The area mainly defined by locations along and north of Interstate 20 and along and east of Interstate 55. By the middle portion of April, rainfall across this area was running some 8 to 12 inches below normal, with the largest deficits occurring across the eastern counties, generally along the Alabama state line.

A potent upper level system and cold front pushed across the region during the early afternoon hours of April 11th. The best moisture and instability were located across eastern Mississippi and into Alabama. As the front clashed into this unstable airmass, scattered thunderstorms began to fire and a few of them became severe. The worst of the severe weather occurred over Alabama where storms were able to organize into several large supercells.
An arctic airmass moved over much of North Mississippi during the early morning hours of April 7, 2007. Record-breaking low temperatures occurred over the area as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Record-breaking low temperatures occurred the morning of April 7, 2007. Lows dropped into the mid to upper 20s which caused considerable crop and vegetation damage. Leaves were literally frozen off of trees. Corn that had six to seven leaves or beyond was killed. Winter wheat suffered the most harm. 75 to 80 percent of the winter wheat crop was destroyed.

Rainfall was about 3 inches below normal for the month bringing the yearly deficit to around 9 inches below normal. The drought impacted agricultural and hydrological interests of the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved out of Northeast Texas and Louisiana into Northwest Mississippi during the evening of April 13, 2007. The system brought a warm front northward into North Mississippi which set off numerous showers and thunderstorms. Many of the thunderstorms became severe with large hail. Storms redeveloped during the early morning hours of April 14, 2007; some of which produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds caused extensive damage to one trailer near Paynes, when the roof and metal siding peeled away from the trailer. Another trailer received substantial damage. In addition, four other homes as well as three farm sheds received minor roof damage. Trees were also knocked down on the grounds of the Paynes Baptist Church and the old Paynes School Grounds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved out of Northeast Texas and Louisiana into Northwest Mississippi during the evening of April 13, 2007. The system brought a warm front northward into North Mississippi which set off numerous showers and thunderstorms. Many of the thunderstorms became severe with large hail. Storms redeveloped during the early morning hours of April 14, 2007; some of which produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds blew a roof off of the back portion of Calhoun Furniture II in Calhoun City. In addition, numerous power lines were also knocked down resulting in a 16 hour power outage for Calhoun City. Also, trees were knocked down in Vardaman Park in the city of Vardaman. The trees damaged some playground equipment.

By the middle portion of April, rainfall across this area was running some 8 to 12 inches below normal, with the largest deficits occurring across the eastern counties, generally along the Alabama state line.

A potent upper level disturbance lifted northeast across the Southern Plains and into the Ohio Valley. As this system evolved and tracked northeast, severe weather broke out across a large portion of the region between April 13th and 14th. Many reports of large hail, damaging winds and a few isolated tornadoes were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured wind gust of 55 MPH in Downtown Jackson as a squall line moved through the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered afternoon showers and thunderstorms developed across the region with a several becoming severe. These storms continued to be uncharacteristic of May, they were more like storms that develop in June or July with little movement and only lasting short periods of time.

During the month of May, the drought worsened and expanded a little as only a spattering of rain occurred across the area with the highest totals  falling along and west of the Mississippi River. Actually, the area defined by severe drought (D2) only expanded by a few counties. The largest expansion was by the area defined by extreme drought (D3), which by the end of the month encompassed an area mainly along and east of Interstate 55 from Grenada to Jackson and along and north of Interstate 20 from Jackson to Meridian. Through May, the entire area within the drought experienced annual rainfall deficits between 12 and 16 inches, (15-25% of normal rainfall), with the driest conditions occurring across Eastern Mississippi.||By the end of May, crops across the state were beginning to feel the impact of the very dry conditions. At that point, some damage to crops had occurred with only small losses to yields expected. Extended drought conditions would make conditions worse with more substantial crop damage/losses if no rain occurred into the month of June.

As a result of below normal rainfall during the month of May, the drought  intensified, and became extreme over Northeast Mississippi and severe over North Central Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe drought(D2) continued into early May across portions of Northeast Mississippi and North Central Mississippi. By the end of the month, the drought had intensified to an extreme drought(D3) over Northeast Mississippi. Rainfall was about 5 inches below normal for the month bringing the yearly deficit to around 13 inches below normal. The drought impacted agricultural and hydrological interests of the area. Burn bans were also put into place to prevent wildfires.

During the month of May, the drought worsened and expanded a little as only a spattering of rain occurred across the area with the highest totals  falling along and west of the Mississippi River. Actually, the area defined by severe drought (D2) only expanded by a few counties. The largest expansion was by the area defined by extreme drought (D3), which by the end of the month encompassed an area mainly along and east of Interstate 55 from Grenada to Jackson and along and north of Interstate 20 from Jackson to Meridian. Through May, the entire area within the drought experienced annual rainfall deficits between 12 and 16 inches, (15-25% of normal rainfall), with the driest conditions occurring across Eastern Mississippi.||By the end of May, crops across the state were beginning to feel the impact of the very dry conditions. At that point, some damage to crops had occurred with only small losses to yields expected. Extended drought conditions would make conditions worse with more substantial crop damage/losses if no rain occurred into the month of June.
As a result of below normal rainfall during the month of May, the drought  intensified, and became extreme over Northeast Mississippi and severe over North Central Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe drought(D2) continued into early May across portions of Northeast Mississippi and North Central Mississippi. By the end of the month, the drought had intensified to an extreme drought(D3) over Northeast Mississippi. Rainfall was about 5 inches below normal for the month bringing the yearly deficit to around 13 inches below normal. The drought impacted agricultural and hydrological interests of the area. Burn bans were also put into place to prevent wildfires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the month of June, the drought peaked across the region. It held firm across the same areas since May with no expansion. What did expand was the severity as by the end of June, most of Central and East-Central Mississippi was now in extreme drought (D3) with some locations across Northeast Mississippi now experiencing exceptional drought (D4). The month of June did not offer much rain as most of the forecast area saw less than 40% of the normal rainfall. If you would go back to the start of the drought, the portion of the forecast area under the drought has only seen 5 to 20% of the annual rainfall. Historical climate sites are running some 15 to 20 inches below normal for rainfall with deficits now higher than the yearly total.| |With the drought conditions reaching extreme levels, area farmers and crops began feeling the effects. Many crops from corn to soybeans have been affected with yields forecast to be much less than normal by harvest time. The quality of the crops has been affected as well due to the dry conditions. The total losses will not be fully know until fall after harvest.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe drought(D2) continued into much of June across portions of North Central Mississippi while an extreme drought(D3) occurred over Northeast Mississippi. By the end of the month, the drought had relaxed over portions of North Central Mississippi while the drought intensified over portions of Northeast Mississippi to an exceptional(D4)drought. Rainfall was about 2 inches below normal for the month bringing the yearly deficit to around 15 inches below normal. The drought impacted agricultural and hydrological interests of the area. Burn bans were also put into place to prevent wildfires.

Many crops from corn to soybeans have been affected with yields forecast to be much less than normal by harvest time. The quality of the crops has been affected as well due to the dry conditions. The total losses will not be fully know until fall after harvest.

As a result of below normal rainfall during the month of June, the drought remained extreme over Northeast Mississippi and severe over North Central Mississippi. Although by the end of the month, some relief occurred over North Central Mississippi while the drought intensified over portions of Northeast Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe drought(D2) continued into much of June across portions of North Central Mississippi while an extreme drought(D3) occurred over Northeast Mississippi. By the end of the month, the drought had relaxed over portions of North Central Mississippi while the drought intensified over portions of Northeast Mississippi to an exceptional(D4)drought. Rainfall was about 2 inches below normal for the month bringing the yearly deficit to around 15 inches below normal. The drought impacted agricultural and hydrological interests of the area. Burn bans were also put into place to prevent wildfires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of Jan 4th and early morning hours of Jan 5th, a potent storm system moved across the Lower Mississippi Valley. This system was dynamic enough to generate a line of thunderstorms across Central Mississippi. This line developed within a strongly sheared environment and evolved into a squall line with bowing segments. These bowing segments proved very efficient with respect to producing damaging wind gusts and six tornadoes. The squall line matured just east of Interstate 55 around 11 pm and raced east before exiting into Alabama around 3 am.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado moved into Neshoba County from Newton County near the Neville Community. Numerous trees were snapped and uprooted. Three homes were damaged with one home having the roof taken off and blown several hundred feet away. Additionally, four chicken houses were destroyed just inside Neshoba County. Total path length across both Newton and Neshoba Counties was 5.3 miles.

Afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms developed over North Mississippi on June 18, 2007 due to daytime heating. Some of the thunderstorms became severe producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were knocked down from straight line winds, 3 miles south of Sabougla. Some of the trees fell on houses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved through North Mississippi during the early morning hours of June 19, 2007. A large complex of showers and thunderstorms developed in association with the disturbance. The showers and thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall which caused flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced 5.3 inches of rain over Panola County, 2 miles east-northeast of Batesville to 12 miles east-northeast of Batesville. The rain caused flash flooding causing 25 roads to become flooded including Highway 6, Spring Port Road, Highway 315, Blackjack Road, Cold Springs Road, and Still Road. Fifteen homes and two businesses were also flooded.

Historical climate sites are running some 15 to 20 inches below normal for rainfall with deficits now higher than the yearly total.| |With the drought conditions reaching extreme levels, area farmers and crops began feeling the effects. Many crops from corn to soybeans have been affected with yields forecast to be much less than normal by harvest time. The quality of the crops has been affected as well due to the dry conditions. The total losses will not be fully know until fall after harvest.

A strong cold front moved into North Mississippi during the late afternoon and evening hours of April 3, 2007. The front produced numerous showers and thunderstorms many of which became severe. Damaging winds and large hail occurred as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to ping pong size hail fell from Tupelo to Verona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the first half of August, a heat wave took hold of the region and brought some of the warmest temperatures since the summer of 2000. This heat wave began around August 5th and lasted until the 16th. Between August 10th and 15th, the entire area reached 100 degrees or higher. Twenty three record highs were also set during this time with one location, Greenwood, tying their all time highest temperature at 106 degrees. In addition to the plain hot temperatures, humidity levels were quite high as well. As the temperature soared each day, high relative humidities resulted in heat index values between 105 and 112 degrees.||This heat wave was responsible for one fatality. The fatality occurred on August 8th, in Mount Olive, MS. A 17 year old male collapsed during football practice, then later passed away at the hospital.||The hot temperatures during the first and middle part of the month helped to contribute to one of the warmest months on record for August. Each of the six climate locations, or stations of record, recorded August temperatures in the top five warmest with two stations recording their warmest August ever. The warmest August ever occurred at Greenville and Hattiesburg.

A severe heat wave brought high temperatures near or above the 100 degree mark during most of the month of August. The unusually warm temperatures combined with high dew points produced heat index values of 105 degrees or greater during the heat wave. One fatality was reported across North Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot and Humid weather settled over North Mississippi during most of August. High temperatures reached near or above the 100 degree mark during the month with heat index values reaching above the 105 degrees.

A severe heat wave brought high temperatures near or above the 100 degree mark during most of the month of August. The unusually warm temperatures combined with high dew points produced heat index values of 105 degrees or greater during the heat wave. One fatality was reported across North Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot and Humid weather settled over North Mississippi during most of August. High temperatures reached near or above the 100 degree mark during the month with heat index values reaching above the 105 degrees.

Typical scattered afternoon thunderstorms developed across the region, mainly across portions of Northeast Mississippi, and produced some severe weather.

An active sea breeze front contained a northward moving line of thunderstorms which produced scattered severe storms across the region. This sea breeze front was much stronger than typical as unusually strong winds were present in the mid levels of the atmosphere. These increased winds allowed the afternoon storms to pack more of a punch as wind damage occurred as they moved north across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down around Columbia and northward to the Bunker Hill and Improve Communities. One tree fell on a house in Columbia with another taking down a power line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an active severe weather day on June 18th, the atmosphere quickly recovered and was able to support additional severe weather which began in the late evening and persisted through the overnight hours. Severe weather during the overnight hours in June is quite rare but does occur on occasion. One of the overnight storms actually produced a rare June tornado! This overnight severe weather was likely a sign and just a taste of what was to come during the day on June 19th. As the overnight/early morning complex of storms shifted east, conditions were set just right for additional storms to redevelop to the west by late morning. The atmosphere recovered once more and became quite unstable in the wake of the morning complex. A very potent disturbance dropped south out of Arkansas and developed a large bow echo complex which plowed south across the forecast area. This bow echo was very efficient at producing severe wind gusts and a large swath of wind damage occurred as the system moved southward across Mississippi into Southeast Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down.

During the first half of August, a heat wave took hold of the region and brought some of the warmest temperatures since the summer of 2000. This heat wave began around August 5th and lasted until the 16th. Between August 10th and 15th, the entire area reached 100 degrees or higher. Twenty three record highs were also set during this time with one location, Greenwood, tying their all time highest temperature at 106 degrees. In addition to the plain hot temperatures, humidity levels were quite high as well. As the temperature soared each day, high relative humidities resulted in heat index values between 105 and 112 degrees.||This heat wave was responsible for one fatality. The fatality occurred on August 8th, in Mount Olive, MS. A 17 year old male collapsed during football practice, then later passed away at the hospital.||The hot temperatures during the first and middle part of the month helped to contribute to one of the warmest months on record for August. The extreme to exceptional drought, which had gripped a large portion of the region since May, finally was reduced during the first and middle portion of July. The drought lasted for only a few days before widespread rains began falling around the 3-4 of July. These rains lasted for nearly 2 weeks until the 21st. Rainfall was nearly a daily occurrence during this time frame with the entire forecast area seeing above normal rainfall for July. The area saw a range to 6 to 11 inches of rain for the month.||Due to the lingering effects of the drought, there were still some agricultural losses during the first few days of July, albeit much less than what occurred during the peak of the drought which occurred during June.

An active sea breeze front contained a northward moving line of thunderstorms which produced scattered severe storms across the region. This sea breeze front was much stronger than typical as unusually strong winds were present in the mid levels of the atmosphere. These increased winds allowed the afternoon storms to pack more of a punch as wind damage occurred as they moved north across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A radio tower, 100 ft tall, was blown down in the Florence area. Additionally, numerous trees were blown down from the Florence/Monterey area to Brandon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active sea breeze front contained a northward moving line of thunderstorms which produced scattered severe storms across the region. This sea breeze front was much stronger than typical as unusually strong winds were present in the mid levels of the atmosphere. These increased winds allowed the afternoon storms to pack more of a punch as wind damage occurred as they moved north across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weakening outflow boundary knocked down one tree on a power line in the city of Madison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an active severe weather day on June 18th, the atmosphere quickly recovered and was able to support additional severe weather which began in the late evening and persisted through the overnight hours. Severe weather during the overnight hours in June is quite rare but does occur on occasion. One of the overnight storms actually produced a rare June tornado! This overnight severe weather was likely a sign and just a taste of what was to come during the day on June 19th. As the overnight/early morning complex of storms shifted east, conditions were set just right for additional storms to redevelop to the west by late morning. The atmosphere recovered once more and became quite unstable in the wake of the morning complex. A very potent disturbance dropped south out of Arkansas and developed a large bow echo complex which plowed south across the forecast area. This bow echo was very efficient at producing severe wind gusts and a large swath of wind damage occurred as the system moved southward across Mississippi into Southeast Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down from just northwest of Lexington, in Lexington and just south of town on Highway 17. A few power lines were blown down as well.

The drought remained extreme over Northeast Mississippi and severe over North Central Mississippi at the beginning of July. However, above normal rainfall during the month, relaxed drought conditions to moderate or abnormally dry over North Central Mississippi and to severe over Northeast Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe drought(D2) continued at the beginning of July across portions of North Central Mississippi while an extreme drought(D3) occurred over Northeast Mississippi. By the end of the month, the drought had relaxed over North Central Mississippi to abnormally dry(D0) or moderate(D1). The drought also relaxed across Northeast Mississippi to severe(D2). Rainfall was about 3 inches above normal for the month reducing the yearly deficit to around 12 inches below normal. The drought continued to impact agricultural and hydrological interests of the area. Although burn bans were lifted due to the recent rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rare early morning storms moved across the Mississippi Delta region and produced locally heavy rainfall. Some flooding occurring during the morning from Lake Washington to Anguilla as storms moved slowly across the area. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow moving thunderstorm produced heavy rain around Lake Washington. Chatham measured 3 inches in an hour with a other locations receiving 4 to 5 inches in 1 to 2 hours. This heavy rain resulted in several area roads under water and numerous fields flooded.

The extreme to exceptional drought, which had gripped a large portion of the region since May, finally was reduced during the first and middle portion of July. The drought lasted for only a few days before widespread rains began falling around the 3-4 of July. These rains lasted for nearly 2 weeks until the 21st. Rainfall was nearly a daily occurrence during this time frame with the entire forecast area seeing above normal rainfall for July. The area saw a range to 6 to 11 inches of rain for the month.||Due to the lingering effects of the drought, there were still some agricultural losses during the first few days of July, albeit much less than what occurred during the peak of the drought which occurred during June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system developed across the central sections of the country and brought two rounds of severe weather to the region. The first occurred during the evening of the 17th and the second during most of the day on the 18th. Wind damage and several tornadoes occurred during this event. This mid October severe weather event was one of the top five largest across the United States.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down along the short path and a storage building had its roof blown off. An addition to a house had a portion of the roof blown off and another storage building was destroyed. The tornado was rated EF1 with a path length of 1/3 mile and a width of 100 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rain fell across across south Mississippi in advance of a deep upper level low pressure system and a strong cold front. Rainfall amounts ranging from three to eight inches were common across Pearl River and Hancock Counties. Street flooding was widespread and a number of vehicles, homes, and businesses were flooded. The worst flooding occurred in the Picayune area where 40 to 60 homes, 5 to 7 businesses, and 3 fire stations were flooded with four to six inches of water. In addition, passengers of a car had to be rescued after it overturned and became submerged in the flooded median of Interstate 59 near Picayune.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor to locally moderate river flooding developed over the next couple of days along the East and West Hobolochitto Rivers following the heavy rainfall that fell over the region. |

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms developed over sections of coastal Mississippi ahead of a developing strong upper level trough.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado destroyed one mobile home and heavily damaged at least ten others in the Lucasville community just west of Vancleave.  In addition, the tornado destroyed several outbuildings, snapped trees, and knocked down power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous slow moving thunderstorms developed across the area during the afternoon and evening of July 20th. A few of these became severe and produced damaging wind gusts. The slow nature of these storms also allowed them to produce locally heavy rainfall with a number of areas seeing 1 to 2 inches in a short amount of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants, of what was briefly hurricane Humberto, moved slowly east northeast across portions of Central and Northeast Mississippi. Across Southwest Mississippi, a couple of storms became severe and produced a little wind damage. The main result from the remnants of Humberto was the locally heavy rainfall that occurred. The heaviest rains fell during the early morning hours of September 14th across Carroll, Montgomery and Webster counties. These three counties received 4 to 6 inches of rain and some flash flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rain, 4 to 6 inches, caused a portion of Highway 35 to flood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month of July offered some relief from the exceptional drought which occurred during the first part of summer. However, hot and dry conditions returned in August and caused a small section of Eastern Mississippi to reach severe drought conditions once again. Luckily, ground moisture from the rains in July kept the drought from expanding or causing much in the way of agricultural losses.
Widespread showers and thunderstorms developed across the area with a few becoming strong enough to produce damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and a few power lines were blown down, generally from Elliott to Grenada. A few trees were blown down at The Dogwoods Golf Course where a tree fell on a golf cart. One minor injury occurred from the fallen tree. Additionally, a few buildings in Grenada sustained some roof damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into Arkansas during the evening hours of October 17th, 2007 into the early morning hours of October 18th, 2007. Showers and thunderstorms developed out ahead of the front. Some of the storms produced damaging winds and flash flooding across North Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven trees were knocked down by straight line winds in a pecan orchard on Slater Road, 1 mile north-northwest of Coahoma.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought re-intensified from extreme(D3) to exceptional (D4) over Northeast Mississippi and from severe(D2) to extreme(D3) over North Central Mississippi during the month of August due to below normal rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe drought(D2) re-intensified to an extreme drought(D3) during the month of August across portions of North Central Mississippi while an extreme drought(D3) re-intensified to exceptional(D4)over Northeast Mississippi. Rainfall was about 2 inches below normal for the month increasing the yearly deficit to around 14 inches below normal. The drought continued to impact agricultural and hydrological interests of the area. Burn bans in some locations were also re-issued due to the lack of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into Arkansas during the evening hours of October 17th, 2007 into the early morning hours of October 18th, 2007. Showers and thunderstorms developed out ahead of the front. Some of the storms produced damaging winds and flash flooding across North Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were knocked down by straight line winds near Highway 305 and College Road, 3 miles south of Olive Branch. In addition, tree limbs fell into a house causing structural damage to the house and some privacy fencing, 2 miles southeast of Olive Branch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe heat wave brought high temperatures near or above the 100 degree mark during most of the month of August. The unusually warm temperatures combined with high dew points produced heat index values of 105 degrees or greater during the heat wave. One fatality was reported across North Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot and Humid weather settled over North Mississippi during most of August. High temperatures reached near or above the 100 degree mark during the month with heat index values reaching above the 105 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much needed rainfall during the month of October continued to relax the drought from severe(D2) and extreme(D3) conditions over extreme North Mississippi to abnormally dry(D0) and moderate(D1) conditions. Severe (D2) conditions remained over extreme Northeast Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Much needed rainfall during the month of October continued to relax the drought from severe(D2) and extreme(D3) conditions over extreme North Mississippi to abnormally dry(D0) and moderate(D1) conditions. Severe (D2) conditions remained over extreme Northeast Mississippi.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Much needed rainfall during the month of October continued to relax the drought from severe(D2) and extreme(D3) conditions over extreme North Mississippi to abnormally dry(D0) and moderate(D1) conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much needed rainfall helped the drought relax from exceptional(D4) to severe (D2) over Northeast Mississippi. However, over extreme North Mississippi, the drought remained severe(D2) to extreme(D3) during the month of September due to below normal rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extreme drought(D3)continued during the month of September across portions of extreme North Mississippi while an exceptional drought(D4) relaxed to severe(D2) over Northeast Mississippi. Rainfall was about 2 inches below normal for the month over extreme North Mississippi while Northeast Mississippi received an inch above normal rainfall. This brought the yearly deficit to around 13 to 17 inches below normal. The drought continued to impact agricultural and hydrological interests of the area. Burn bans in some locations also remained in effect due to the lack of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system developed across the central sections of the country and brought two rounds of severe weather to the region. The first occurred during the evening of the 17th and the second during most of the day on the 18th. Wind damage and several tornadoes occurred during this event. This mid October severe weather event was one of the top five largest across the United States.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado touched down along Highway 39 just s of the Kemper/Noxubee county line. Here several branches and tree debris was blown across the road. Just to the northeast, several large pine trees were snapped and uprooted and blown across a road. The tornado continued to the northeast, just into Noxubee county where several more trees were blown down. Total path length was 3 miles across both counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of Jan 4th and early morning hours of Jan 5th, a potent storm system moved across the Lower Mississippi Valley. This system was dynamic enough to generate a line of thunderstorms across Central Mississippi. This line developed within a strongly sheared environment and evolved into a squall line with bowing segments. These bowing segments proved very efficient with respect to producing damaging wind gusts and six tornadoes. The squall line matured just east of Interstate 55 around 11 pm and raced east before exiting into Alabama around 3 am.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down between Mendenhall and Magee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved across the Plains and Mid-West late on April 3rd and during the morning hours of April 4th. In advance of this system, scattered severe thunderstorms developed over Mississippi during the afternoon hours of April 3rd. This activity only lasted for a few hours during the late afternoon. However, across the Mid-South region, numerous severe thunderstorms developed and pushed south through the evening. After midnight, these storms began to weaken, but several remained severe as the complex pushed south across Mississippi. Scattered severe storms continued until around 4 am before the complex dissipated.

A strong upper level trough moved through North Mississippi during the early morning hours of December 28th, 2007. Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with the trough. Some of the thunderstorms became severe and produced large hail.

A potent upper level disturbance lifted northeast across the Southern Plains and into the Ohio Valley. As this system evolved and tracked northeast, severe weather broke out across a large portion of the region between April 13th and 14th. Many reports of large hail, damaging winds and a few isolated tornadoes were received.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down several trees and power lines in the city of Corinth. In addition, one of the knocked down trees fell on a home and produced roof damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active sea breeze front contained a northward moving line of thunderstorms which produced scattered severe storms across the region. This sea breeze front was much stronger than typical as unusually strong winds were present in the mid levels of the atmosphere. These increased winds allowed the afternoon storms to pack more of a punch as wind damage occurred as they moved north across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down along with one large limb on a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe weather developed over the region during the day on May 3rd, peaked during the late afternoon hours and ended by early evening. The majority of the severe weather, as well as the hardest hit areas, occurred across Southeast Arkansas, Northeast Louisiana and West-Central Mississippi. A mix of hail, damaging winds and a few tornadoes occurred during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree and a few power lines blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another powerful spring storm system developed over the plains and spread eastward causing severe weather from the Mid West to the south-central Gulf Coast states to the Southeast United States. Overall, the Lower Mississippi Valley was spared the brunt of the worst weather with just scattered reports of severe weather. However, the  Southeast United States was hit quite hard as significant severe storms occurred. This region had many reports of large hail and damaging winds with several strong tornadoes (EF2 to EF3) and a few violent tornadoes (EF4).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down off Chapel Hill Road.
